original_abstract: Measuring the perception of visual content is a long-standing problem in computer vision. Many mathematical models have been developed to evaluate the look or quality of an image. Despite the effectiveness of such tools in quantifying degradations such as noise and blurriness levels, such quantification is loosely coupled with human language. When it comes to more abstract perception about the feel of visual content, existing methods can only rely on supervised models that are explicitly trained with labeled data collected via laborious user study. In this paper, we go beyond the conventional paradigms by exploring the rich visual language prior encapsulated in Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models for assessing both the quality perception (look) and abstract perception (feel) of images without explicit task-specific training. In particular, we discuss effective prompt designs and show an effective prompt pairing strategy to harness the prior. We also provide extensive experiments on controlled datasets and Image Quality Assessment (IQA) benchmarks. Our results show that CLIP captures meaningful priors that generalize well to different perceptual assessments.
humanized_abstract: Image and video processing are often conducted in the context of human perception. So understanding how an image is seen by the human eye is fundamental. Image quality assessment is a mathematical approach that tries to model and quantify how an image looks. Quality metrics quantify artifacts like noise and blur and work well within their assumption. They have only a loose relationship with human language and are not directly linked to the more subjective metrics that model how we feel when interacting with a certain visual content. Assessing more abstract notions about the feel of the visual content is traditionally done with supervised learning, where the training dataset is based on scores collected by showing images to people. This can be very expensive and time-consuming, which creates a very high cost on requiring only a minimum amount of information to be collected from users in order for the application to be as efficient as possible. In this paper we try to go a step further from traditional work and instead of only using a trained model we investigate how we can use knowledge included in an already trained model of a CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) algorithm that have a more general meaning on how an image or content should look, so that we do not need more data about the feeling people have about the quality of certain images, which is a costly data to be obtained from users, to obtain good results from the proposed model. We discuss possible prompts and show with experimental results that we have a method to design pairs of prompts that yields good results, for several commonly used quality assessment benchmarks and datasets.
